fingerprint: The 18.613-year nodal period is a three-body gravitational quantity derived from M_Sun, M_Earth, M_Moon, and Keplerian orbital parameters. The amplitude of the associated lunar declination cycle (±18.3° to ±28.6°) directly encodes the globe's 23.44° axial obliquity and the Moon's 5.145° orbital inclination — both spherical geometry quantities with no flat-disc derivation.
globe_value: Period: T = 2π/Ω̇ where Ω̇ depends on n_Sun²/n_Moon × cos(i_Moon) — all Keplerian quantities. Amplitude: 23.44° ± 5.145° (obliquity ± lunar inclination) defines the declination envelope.
dome_value: Period asserted as 18.6 years via 'epicyclic gears' — no gear ratios, no derivation from dome parameters. Amplitude not addressed. The only moon_dec formula in the codebase uses a FIXED 28.6° amplitude with 27.3-day period — it does not model the 18.6-year modulation at all.
match_precision: Exact adoption — the dome uses the standard-physics value 18.6 years without any independent derivation path from disc_radius, firmament_height, or any dome parameter.
why_it_matters: The 18.613-year period is derived from perturbation theory applied to three gravitationally interacting bodies in Keplerian orbits around a sphere. No path exists from flat-disc geometry to this specific period. The dome model cannot even state WHAT determines the gear ratios — because the answer is 'the Sun/Earth/Moon mass ratios and orbital elements,' which are globe-framework quantities. Furthermore, the observable consequence (lunar declination modulation between 18.3° and 28.6°) encodes 23.44° obliquity — the same globe parameter the dome borrows in WIN-056's latitude formula. This is a double borrowing: both the period and its amplitude are globe-derived.
suggested_text: The 18.613-year nodal period is a three-body gravitational quantity: it arises from the Sun's torque on the Moon's inclined orbit, derived through perturbation theory by Clairaut in 1749–50 using M<sub>Sun</sub>, M<sub>Earth</sub>, M<sub>Moon</sub>, and their Keplerian orbital elements. The dome model's 'epicyclic gears' specify no gear ratios and provide no derivation from dome parameters (disc radius, firmament height, etc.). The observable amplitude — lunar declination oscillating between ±18.3° and ±28.6° — directly encodes the globe's 23.44° axial obliquity (the same constant borrowed in WIN-056) plus the Moon's 5.145° orbital inclination to the ecliptic, a concept that does not exist in flat-disc geometry.

code_search.found_in: inject_ai_layer.py: WIN-020 entry in confirmed_wins array — title only ('Lunar declination 18.6-year cycle', data_source 'Gear mechanics'). predictions.js: identical entry. api/current/formulas.json: FORM-041 and FORM-125 define moon_dec = 28.6 * sin(2π*days/27.3 + 1.2) — a MONTHLY oscillation with FIXED 28.6° amplitude. Source files (dome_predictor_interactive.py, v19_pipeline.py) DO NOT EXIST in the repository.
code_search.monitoring: none — monitor.py does not track WIN-020
code_search.critical_finding: The only moon declination formula in the entire codebase (FORM-041/FORM-125) models the 27.3-day MONTHLY oscillation with a constant 28.6° amplitude. It does NOT model the 18.6-year nodal cycle at all — the amplitude is hardcoded at its MAXIMUM value (23.44° + 5.145°), ignoring the very phenomenon WIN-020 claims to reproduce. No epicyclic gear model, no gear ratios, no 18.613-year period computation exists anywhere in the repository. The 'epicyclic gear mechanics' description in WIN-020 is an assertion with zero implementation.
code_search.notes: 28.6° = 23.44° obliquity + 5.145° lunar inclination — both globe-derived quantities. The formula source files (dome_predictor_interactive.py, v19_pipeline.py) are referenced in the API database but do not exist in the repository, suggesting they were generated for documentation rather than functional code.
